query: chest x-ray findings in croup vs foreign body aspiration

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Often times, foreign bodies in the upper trachea can be mistaken for viral croup as an acute aspiration may cause symptoms of stridor. Additionally, a foreign body that is lodged in the esophagus of a younger child may cause stridor simply secondary to mass effect on the trachea. A lingering foreign body may present as a nidus for recurrent pneumonia in the long-term setting. Asthma and viral upper respiratory tract infections can obscure the diagnosis. In one study, 34% of patients who underwent flexible bronchoscopy but were found not to have a foreign body had persistent abnormal chest radiographs. In these selected patients, the most common finding on chest radiographs was bronchial thickening. Symptoms of concurrent respiratory infection were present in 42.5% of these patients. [11] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In a review of common imaging modalities for diagnosis of foreign body aspiration, a simple algorithm based on feasibility and utility was generated at one institution. Recommendations included initial diagnostic work-up with frontal and lateral chest films with additional neck films if warranted on exam.  Abnormalities at this stage would be sufficient to necessitate bronchoscopy. However, if the radiographs are unrevealing in the symptomatic patient with a suggestive history, a CT of the chest should subsequently be performed. If CT is unavailable and the child is > 5  years of age, then inspiratory-expiratory films can be obtained. If the child is younger than 5 years old, bilateral decubitus films are preferred. Fluoroscopy and MRI can be used as adjunctive measures. It should be noted that this was an algorithm formulated at one particular institution and is not universal practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: To mitigate the potential need for CT scan, one study has evaluated a quantitative method to increase the sensitivity of radiographs to detect foreign bodies, in particular aspirated objects which are radiolucent. In a case-control study, radiodensity ratios were compared between the affected lung and the contralateral lung in patients with definitively diagnosed foreign body aspiration and healthy controls. The radiodensity ratio was seen to be significantly higher in in patients with aspiration as compared to controls. Radiodensity was calculated for each lung by measuring total Hounsfield units and then the ratio developed involved comparison between the affected lung and the normal lung. The authors of this study suggest a radiodensity ratio cutoff of 1.10 is sufficiently positive to warrant further investigation with bronchoscopy, whereas a ratio below this value would warrant further work-up with a CT scan. As there is a relative paucity evaluating this method in the literature, further studies are needed for validation. [5] [6] [7] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may be completely asymptomatic and the only evidence of an aspiration event may be found during history taking. However, sudden onset of cough, choking, and/or dyspnea have been found to be the most common symptoms. One prospective study has cited a sensitivity of 91.1% and specificity of 45.2% for choking and acute cough. Wheeze on auscultation has been found to be a major physical finding and in one study was documented in 60% of cases. In the same study, 32% of patients had asymmetric breath sounds. An abnormal physical exam has been seen to have a sensitivity of 80.4% and specificity of 59.5% for foreign body aspiration. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Differential Diagnosis -- Foreign Body
retrieved_docs.content: A previously healthy child presents with a sudden onset of choking and upper airway obstruction symptoms. A foreign body lodged in the larynx causes hoarseness and stridor. A large foreign body in the upper esophagus can pressurize the extra-thoracic trachea, resulting in a barking cough and inspiratory stridor. Late onset of stridor can occur due to the ingestion of a nonobstructive erosive foreign body, such as a button battery. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngomalacia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Foreign body aspiration is a possibility after finding an infant in respiratory distress after being unaccompanied or after ingesting food, causing a choking or coughing event. Diagnosis is suggested with chest x-ray findings and decreased unilateral breath sounds. Bronchoscopy should be performed to diagnose and retrieve the foreign body.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Chest radiographs are often utilized as initial tool of investigation for foreign body aspiration. Radiological findings consistent with a foreign body aspiration include atelectasis, pneumothorax, and air trapping. However, chest radiographs have been seen in multiple studies to be frequently normal, with one study citing a normal chest radiograph in 35% of cases of foreign body aspiration. This same study indicated that the most common abnormality found on radiograph was air trapping, present in 53% of cases. The sensitivity of chest radiograph has been documented as 67.9% and the specificity has been seen to be 71.4%. In one study, children with a concerning history of sudden cough, abnormal pulmonary exam, and abnormal chest x-ray have been shown to have a risk of 88.6% (P<0.001) for foreign body aspiration. It is important to note that rarely a single finding or historical piece of information can definitively diagnose a foreign body aspiration. Rather, direct identification via bronchoscope is often needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History of aspiration or suspected aspiration is often sufficient to warrant a full workup including a rigid bronchoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: According to one study, when a combination of three or more highly suggestive clinical and radiological diagnostic data were noted, the risk of identifying a foreign body in the airway was 91%. Diagnostic clinical criteria significantly correlated with the presence of a foreign body included: a history of sudden choking, cyanosis, apnea, and decreased breath sounds. Diagnostic radiological criteria included: atelectasis, mediastinal shift, or signs of air trapping.  The absence of the aforementioned criteria was very predictive of a negative flexible bronchoscopy. Thus, it was deemed safe to refer these patients for outpatient follow-up as opposed to initial bronchoscopy. [6] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of croup includes other causes of stridor and respiratory distress, such as bacterial tracheitis, epiglottitis, foreign body aspiration, hemangioma, peritonsillar abscess, neoplasm, retropharyngeal abscess, and smoke inhalation. Rarely, neurological causes can cause stridor that mimics croup. In a report, 3 children presented with stridor and were treated for croup but were eventually found to have a neurological cause of stridor. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheomalacia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with foreign body aspiration usually have a history of choking after swallowing an object. Plain radiographs will show the foreign body if it is radio-opaque. Ventilating bronchoscopy may be both diagnostic and therapeutic for these patients. It is particularly important to determine if the foreign body is of organic material or a button battery, as both can significantly damage the respiratory epithelial lining.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of FBAO is typically based on history and physical examination. Radiographs may help confirm the diagnosis but should not be solely relied upon to exclude FBAO. For example, chest radiographs often appear normal or may show abnormalities that are not typical of foreign body aspiration in children with airway foreign bodies. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is stable and cooperative, evaluation can begin with chest X-rays, PA, and lateral inspiratory-expiratory films. Unilateral expansion with diaphragm flattening of the affected side with mediastinal pushing away will indicate obstruction on that side. Air trapping can also be demonstrated on inspiratory-expiratory films indicative of a ball-valve effect due to a foreign body obstruction. If the foreign body is radiopaque, these can also identify and localize the object. Obtain IV access with bloodwork including complete blood count and electrolytes in preparation for a possible general anesthetic. A routine venous blood gas is of little use and should be avoided. If the patient is stable and cooperative, a head and neck exam with or without flexible laryngoscopy can elucidate other mimicking conditions such as tonsillitis or peritonsillar abscess, which can then be addressed. Auscultation in the primary lung fields can demonstrate wheezing or focal consolidations when paired with percussion and tactile fremitus evaluation. Computed tomography should be reserved for only the most stable and comfortable patients in whom foreign body is very low on the differential diagnosis. An airway emergency in a radiology suite carries a high mortality. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchiolitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma Bacterial pneumonia Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) Vascular ring Croup Foreign body aspiration Pertussis

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid diagnosis and retrieval lead to the best outcomes in these patients

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Interventional pulmonology, otolaryngology, cardiothoracic surgery

retrieved_docs.content: of intraluminal foreign bodies from the trachea or bronchus, fiberoptic bronchoscopic removal, and the broader method of non‑operative foreign‑body extraction all share a common “removal technique” methodology, providing standard care without surgery. Inhalation or ingestion of food leading to airway obstruction (food aspiration, “شفط الطعام”) maps to suffocation e‑codes and also results in pulmonary involvement, as aspirated material can lodge in the lung. Surgical interventions in the lung (“العمليات الجراحية في الرئة”) are considered a subcategory of bronchoscopy (تنظير القصبات), indicating that many thoracic surgeries are performed using or derived from bronchoscopic techniques that offer direct visualization and minimally invasive access to the airways and pulmonary tissue. Recognizing bronchoscopy as the overarching procedural category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic bronchoscopic approaches, aligning procedural choice with the intended

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi constitute a procedural category that includes several distinct therapeutic and diagnostic interventions. Within this category, Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea is performed to extract objects lodged in the airway. Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (initial) is used to clear secretions or debris and to assess airway pathology at the start of a hospital admission. A subsequent Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (subsequent, same hospital stay) provides follow‑up clearance or reassessment during the same admission. Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus involves bronchoscopic removal of abnormal tissue such as tumors or polyps. Recognizing that these four procedures are child procedures of the broader Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi helps clinicians organize airway management strategies, select the appropriate technique for foreign body removal, aspiration, or lesion excision, and coordinate sequential care when multiple bronchoscopic interventions are required during a single hospital stay.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2144
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy, and nonoperative removal of foreign body all employ a removal technique as the method used to extract the foreign object.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2919
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy is performed using a specific removal technique, and nonoperative removal of foreign body likewise employs a removal technique. Both procedures are categorized under removal techniques for foreign bodies, highlighting minimally invasive methods for extracting intraluminal objects from the airway without surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1567
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, fiberoptic bronchoscopic removal of foreign body, removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy, and the method of nonoperative removal of foreign body all employ a removal technique. These procedures represent a non‑surgical approach to extracting foreign objects lodged within the bronchial tree, utilizing bronchoscopy—particularly fiberoptic bronchoscopy—as the primary method. The shared removal technique underscores the importance of endoscopic methods in safely managing airway foreign bodies without the need for operative intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1563
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi encompass several distinct therapeutic interventions. One such intervention is Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance when performed, with therapeutic aspiration of the tracheobronchial tree (initial). Another procedure within this category is Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus, which involves endoscopic removal of abnormal tissue. A further specific technique is Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy, a targeted approach to extract objects lodged within the bronchial airway. Together, these procedures represent the range of endoscopic modalities used to diagnose and treat conditions affecting the trachea and bronchi.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from the trachea, and nonoperative removal of foreign body are all procedures that employ specific removal techniques. These removal techniques provide minimally invasive, non‑surgical methods for extracting foreign objects from the airway, helping to restore patency while reducing patient morbidity and the need for operative intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 107
retrieved_docs.content: terminology aligns these concepts: تنظيم القصبات denotes bronchial or airway management procedures, while عمليات جراحية في الرئة (lung surgeries) are placed under this parent category. Overall, the knowledge base integrates diagnostic and therapeutic bronchoscopy, airway foreign‑body extraction, inflammatory and malignant lung diseases, and their anatomical sites, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing tracheobronchial and pulmonary conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, initial, bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, subsequent, same hospital stay, and endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus are all designated as child procedures of the broader category Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi. These child procedures represent specific therapeutic endoscopic interventions targeting the airway: extraction of foreign bodies, aspiration of tracheobronchial contents during an initial session, repeat aspiration during a subsequent session within the same hospital stay, and excision of bronchial lesions. Recognizing these interventions as subtypes of Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi aids clinicians in selecting appropriate airway management techniques, ensures accurate procedural coding, and supports comprehensive patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2874
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi comprise a group of related therapeutic and diagnostic interventions. Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body is a child procedure within this category, specifically designed to extract inhaled objects from the airway. Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, initial, is another child procedure, involving either rigid or flexible bronchoscopy aided by fluoroscopic imaging and the aspiration of secretions from the tracheobronchial tree. Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus is also a child procedure under this umbrella, focusing on the removal of pathological lesions from the bronchial passages. Collectively, these child procedures illustrate the range of endoscopic techniques employed to diagnose and treat conditions affecting the trachea and bronchi.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1896
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea, and removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy are all procedures that employ a specific removal technique. This removal technique is also identified as the method of nonoperative removal of foreign body, indicating that these airway interventions use minimally invasive, non‑surgical approaches—typically bronchoscopy or endoscopic methods—to extract foreign objects from the trachea or bronchus. The shared use of this removal technique underscores its role as a standard, nonoperative method for managing intraluminal foreign bodies in the respiratory tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi constitute a group of airway endoscopic interventions that include several distinct therapeutic techniques. One of these techniques is Endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from trachea, which involves using an endoscope to extract objects lodged within the tracheal lumen. Another procedure within this group is Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, subsequent, same hospital stay, a comprehensive approach that combines visual inspection, imaging assistance, and aspiration to clear secretions or debris from the tracheobronchial tree during a single admission. The category also encompasses Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus, a targeted endoscopic resection aimed at removing pathological lesions located in the bronchial airways. Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities offered by endoscopic techniques on the trachea and bronchi, highlighting their importance in managing airway foreign bodies, performing guided bronchoscopic interventions, and excising bronchial lesions within a unified clinical framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1399
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy Procedures on the Trachea and Bronchi comprise a set of related bronchoscopic interventions. The category includes “Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, initial,” which describes an initial rigid or flexible bronchoscopy performed with fluoroscopic guidance to therapeutically aspirate the tracheobronchial tree. It also includes “Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, subsequent, same hospital stay,” indicating a repeat bronchoscopy with the same therapeutic aspiration performed during the same hospitalization. In addition, the category contains “Endoscopy with excision of lesion from bronchus,” a bronchoscopic procedure used to excise a lesion within the bronchial tree. Finally, it includes “Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy,” which describes the use of bronchoscopy to extract a foreign object lodged inside the bronchus. Together, these child procedures illustrate the various therapeutic applications of bronchoscopy—ranging from aspiration and lesion removal to foreign‑body extraction—under the broader umbrella of endoscopic procedures on the trachea and bronchi.

Airway foreign‑body management—encompassing endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign bodies from the trachea or bronchus and fiberoptic bronchoscopic removal—utilizes non‑operative removal methods. Inhalation or ingestion of food causing airway obstruction (شفط الطعام) is associated with suffocation coding and also maps to the lung as the finding site. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1806
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, initial, and Bronchoscopy, rigid or flexible, including fluoroscopic guidance, when performed; with therapeutic aspiration of tracheobronchial tree, subsequent, same hospital stay, are both narrower forms of إجراء علاجي (therapeutic procedure). Nuclear medicine procedure is also classified as an إجراء علاجي. These connections indicate that both interventional bronchoscopy with therapeutic aspiration and nuclear medicine interventions are considered therapeutic modalities, highlighting their role in patient treatment and care planning.
